text:
CT UROGRAM WITH IV CONTRAST
CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS (THREE-PHASE PROTOCOL)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Right flank pain and hematuria (trace blood on urinalysis) in a 49-year-old woman with a history of FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, status post primary debulking surgery (R0 resection) and adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy, currently on niraparib maintenance therapy. Referred by her oncologist to evaluate for urologic etiology of right flank discomfort and to exclude disease recurrence.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphase CT urogram of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice multidetector CT scanner following administration of 100 mL Omnipaque 350 (iohexol) intravenous contrast. Three acquisition phases were obtained: corticomedullary phase (40 seconds), nephrographic phase (90 seconds), and delayed urographic phase (10 minutes post-injection) to opacify the collecting systems and ureters. Coronal and sagittal reformats were generated and reviewed. Multiplanar reconstructions in the coronal plane were used for assessment of the ureters in their entirety. No oral contrast administered. Radiation dose: CTDIvol 12.4 mGy; DLP 524 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT of the abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast performed approximately 6 months previously (surveillance scan at 24 months from completion of chemotherapy), which demonstrated no evidence of disease recurrence.

--- FINDINGS ---

CHEST (INCLUDED FOR COMPLETENESS ON DELAYED PHASE):
The lung bases are clear. There are no pleural effusions. No discrete pulmonary nodules or masses are identified at the lung bases. No pericardial effusion.

KIDNEYS:
The right kidney measures 11.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension and demonstrates normal cortical thickness with smooth contours. Enhancement is symmetric and homogeneous across all phases without focal cortical defect. Within the right lower pole collecting system, there is a 4 mm hyperdense focus measuring approximately 550 HU on unenhanced series, consistent with a small calculus situated within the lower pole calyceal fornix. There is no associated dilation of the collecting system, and the right ureter is not dilated. No surrounding perinephric stranding or fluid collection to suggest obstruction or acute obstructive uropathy. No additional calculi are identified within the right kidney on any phase. No solid cortical mass or renal parenchymal lesion. No perinephric lymphadenopathy.

The left kidney measures 10.9 cm in craniocaudal dimension with normal parenchymal thickness and homogeneous enhancement. No calculi identified. No hydronephrosis. No filling defects within the left renal collecting system on urographic phase images. No cortical mass.

URETERS:
Bilateral ureters are opacified on delayed phase imaging and are normal in caliber throughout their course from the renal pelves to the ureterovesical junctions. No filling defects, strictures, or luminal irregularities are identified. No periureteral soft tissue thickening or encasement. The right ureter courses normally through the retroperitoneum and pelvis without deviation or extrinsic compression.

BLADDER:
The urinary bladder is adequately distended and demonstrates smooth, thin walls without focal thickening or intraluminal filling defects. The bladder mucosa enhances uniformly. No stones within the bladder lumen. Trigone appears unremarkable. Post-void images not obtained.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology and attenuation. No nodularity or focal masses identified.

LIVER, SPLEEN, AND PANCREAS:
The liver is normal in size with smooth margins and homogeneous attenuation. No focal parenchymal lesions or hypervascular deposits identified. No intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent. The spleen is normal in size (10.1 cm) and homogeneous in attenuation. The pancreas demonstrates normal morphology and enhancement throughout the head, body, and tail without ductal dilation or focal abnormality. No peripancreatic fluid.

RETROPERITONEUM AND LYMPH NODES:
No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Previously described post-surgical changes in the right obturator fossa have fully resolved; no lymphocele is seen on this study. The para-aortic and paracaval nodal regions are clear, without any discrete soft tissue nodal masses. The aorta and inferior vena cava appear normal in caliber.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
The peritoneal surfaces are smooth throughout the visualized abdomen and pelvis. There is no evidence of peritoneal thickening, plaque-like deposits, or nodular implants along the parietal or visceral peritoneum. The mesentery is without nodularity or infiltration. The omental region, given prior infracolic omentectomy, demonstrates expected post-surgical fatty tissue without any discrete omental nodularity or soft tissue density to suggest recurrent omental disease. No free intra-abdominal fluid or ascites.

PELVIS:
Post-operative changes from prior total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy are again noted, including absence of the uterus and ovaries in their expected locations, and mild soft tissue stranding along the vaginal cuff, which appears similar in extent to prior imaging and is consistent with post-surgical fibrosis. The vaginal cuff is without nodular thickening or adjacent soft tissue masses. No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. No free pelvic fluid. The sigmoid colon and rectum are unremarkable. No pelvic sidewall lesions identified.

BONES:
The visualized osseous structures including the spine, ribs (lung base), and pelvis are without suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions. No cortical destruction. Mild degenerative changes of the lower lumbar spine are noted, within expected limits for age.

--- IMPRESSION ---

1. Small 4 mm calculus within the right lower pole calyces without associated hydronephrosis, hydroureter, or obstructive nephropathy. Given the patient's symptom of right flank discomfort and trace hematuria, this calculus represents the most likely etiology of her presenting symptoms. No additional calculi identified bilaterally.

2. No upper tract urothelial filling defects or masses bilaterally. The bladder wall is smooth and without intraluminal lesion. The ureters are patent and of normal caliber throughout.

3. No evidence of recurrent or new peritoneal disease, omental implants, pelvic masses, or ascites. The peritoneal surfaces remain clean on this examination.

4. No retroperitoneal or pelvic lymphadenopathy. Resolution of previously noted right obturator lymphocele.

5. Expected post-hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy changes with no concerning pelvic soft tissue lesions.

6. No parenchymal hepatic, splenic, or adrenal lesions identified.

Overall, the findings are entirely attributable to a benign right lower pole renal calculus and are unrelated to the patient's underlying ovarian cancer history. There is no imaging evidence of malignant disease recurrence on this examination.

Electronically signed by: Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division
